title: Relate division to multiplication word problems
description: Find both the multiplication and division equation that can be used to solve a word problem.
topic_path: Relate division to multiplication word problems

assessment_items.question_content:
Every group at magic camp is made up of $7$ kids.  There are $56$ total kids at magic camp.


**Which equations would help us find out how many groups of kids are at magic camp?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
There are $\purpleC7$ kids in each group.

There are $\greenD{56}$ total kids.

[[☃ image 1]]



How many $\blueD{\text{groups}}$ are there?

We can write this as a multiplication problem. 

$\purpleC{\text{size of the group}}\times\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}$

So, $\purpleC7\times\blueD{\text{?}} = \greenD{56}$

We can also write this as a division problem. 

$\greenD{\text{total}}\div\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}=\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} $

So, $\greenD{56}\div \purpleC7=\blueD{\text{?}}$

The equations that would help us find out how many groups of kids are at magic camp are:  

 -   $\purpleC7\times\blueD{\text{?}} = \greenD{56}$
 - $\greenD{56}\div \purpleC7=\blueD{\text{?}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
The school store has $54$ pencils to sell.  The store sells all pencils in packs of $6$.  

**Which equations would help us find out how many packs of pencils the store has?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
There are $\purpleC6$ pencils in a pack.

There are $\greenD{54}$ total pencils.

[[☃ image 1]]



How many $\blueD{\text{groups}}$ are there?

We can write this as a multiplication problem. 

$\purpleC{\text{size of the group}}\times\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}$

So, $\purpleC6\times\blueD{\text{?}} = \greenD{54}$

We can also write this as a division problem. 

$\greenD{\text{total}}\div\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}=\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} $

So, $\greenD{54}\div \purpleC6=\blueD{\text{?}}$

The equations that would help us find out how many pack of pencils there are:  

 -   $\purpleC6\times\blueD{\text{?}} = \greenD{54}$
 - $\greenD{54}\div \purpleC6=\blueD{\text{?}}$

$\purpleC{\text{size of the group}}\times\blueD{\text{ number of groups}} = \greenD{\text{ total}}$

We can also write this as a division problem.

assessment_items.question_content:
Catie Clown made the same number of balloon animals for each of the $6$ kids at the birthday party.  She made $24$ balloon animals.

**Which equations would help us find out how many balloon animals Catie Clown made for each kid?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
[[☃ image 1]]

There are $\blueD{6}$ kids.

There are $\greenD{24}$ total balloon animals.


What is the $\purpleC{\text{size of the groups}}$?

We can write this as a multiplication problem. 

$\blueD{\text{ number of groups}}\times\purpleC{\text{size of the group}}= \greenD{\text{ total}}$

So, $\blueD{6}\times\purpleC{\text{?}} = \greenD{24}$

We can also write this as a division problem. 

$\greenD{\text{total}}\div\blueD{\text{ number of groups}}=\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}$

So, $\greenD{24}\div \blueD{6}=\purpleC{\text{?}}$

The equations that would help us find out how many balloon animals Catie Clown made for each kid are: 

- $\blueD{6}\times\purpleC{\text{?}} = \greenD{24}$
- $\greenD{24}\div \blueD{6}=\purpleC{\text{?}}$

assessment_items.question_content:
There are $27$ students in art class.  For the sculpture project, the students are split into $9$ groups.  Each group has the same number of students.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:

What is the $\purpleC{\text{size of the groups}}$?

We can write this as a multiplication problem.

We can also write this as a division problem. 

$\greenD{\text{total}}\div\blueD{\text{ number of groups}}=\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}$

So, $\greenD{27}\div \blueD{9}=\purpleC{\text{?}}$

The equations that would help us find out how many students are in each group are:  

 - $\blueD{9}\times\purpleC{\text{?}} = \greenD{27}$
 - $\greenD{27}\div \blueD{9}=\purpleC{\text{?}}$

assessment_items.question_content:

assessment_items.question_content:
What a great game!  The Rangers scored $6$ goals in the hockey game.  They scored the same number of goals in each of the $3$ periods.  

**Which equations would help us find out how many goals the Rangers scored in each period?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
There are $\blueD{3}$ periods.

The Rangers score $\greenD{6}$ total goals.

[[☃ image 1]]

What is the $\purpleC{\text{size of the groups}}$?

We can write this as a multiplication problem. 

$\blueD{\text{ number of groups}}\times\purpleC{\text{size of the group}}= \greenD{\text{ total}}$

So, $\blueD{3}\times\purpleC{\text{?}} = \greenD{6}$

We can also write this as a division problem. 

$\greenD{\text{total}}\div\blueD{\text{ number of groups}}=\purpleC{\text{ size of the groups}}$

So, $\greenD{6}\div \blueD{3}=\purpleC{\text{?}}$

The equations that would help us find out how many goals the Rangers scored in each period are:  

 - $\blueD{3}\times\purpleC{\text{?}} = \greenD{6}$
 - $\greenD{6}\div \blueD{3}=\purpleC{\text{?}}$
